author: @techniquetechnology2598
text: Can you talk a little about the environmental impact of the particular carbon you at FWG source/produce? Carbon has been criticized as requiring high temperatures to make and thus having a negative overall impact. Some, such as MIT, are on a search for a 'carbon-less' capacitor. Will larger-scale production of your batteries create significant CO2 emissions? Is the carbon you use newly produced, or is it recycled in some way? I am backer #65.

author: @RWBHere
text:
I once stood an unscored, and partly water-filled, milk bottle in a sink of hot water. The result was a perfectly-separated pair of bottle halves. Did it by accident, when I was 13, didn't understand why it happened, until some hours later (no Google, or internet, in 1969. Had to read books!), and never managed to do the same trick again.
Your ink method is much more repeatable! 
Thanks for the demo, Rob.

author: @tracyvaughan9691
text: Great video. Thanks

author: @jamescunningham6017
text:
Would you be able to make it silicone based? So you could have heatable silicone.
1 idea is having silicone mixed with magnetic nano particle so you could have a silicone ball that would heat up in an induction heater. 
Another is having a silicone ball that could be heated up with wires. 
the reason being if you needed to heat something you could shape the silicone to what needed to be heated.

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: Do you mean Silicon? Silicone is a very different thing. Silicon is the element which is used in electronics, whereas silicone is a compound which is often used as a lubricant.

replies.author: @jamescunningham6017
replies.text: no Im talking about making a silicone putty with it. Something you can shape and then apply voltage/current to. That way makes it extremely flexible so people could heat things it what ever way they wanted.

author: @stemcampprojects7951
text:
Another fun video!  I wonder what other crazy thing you'll think of next to do with the ink?  I suggest an egg incubator... wouldn't it be fun to have cute chicks running all over the lab? <grin>  Don't worry about the people complaining about the sound -- sure it would have less of an echo with a lapel mic, but unless your camera accepts the audio input there are also recording and sync issues.  If it does accept an audio input, then even something simple like this: https://www.walmart.com/ip/Pyle-Pro-VHF-Wireless-Microphone-Receiver-System-Independent-Volume-Control-Handheld-Microphone-Belt-Pack-Transmitter-Lavalier-Microphone/43863485?athcpid=43863485&athpgid=athenaItemPage&athcgid=null&athznid=PWVUB&athieid=v0&athstid=CS002&athguid=466001f5-f95b4146-95e03b7f5bfc531c&athena=true#read-more could help cheap.  

I have an off-topic question, if you have just a second.  I'm trying to judge the quality of carbonization for a given material -- trying to tweak parameters to get the best carbonization without actually building the material into batteries until I'm getting close.  It is reasonable to think that the best carbonization for a given material for energy storage devices is likely to be the most conductive?  (I'm thinking the surface area probably doesn't vary widely with the same material)  So - is it reasonable for me to test results by looking for the most conductive powder?

THANKS! Leo Wadsworth

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is surface area that matters first mate - then surface modification for functional groups

author: @bren106

A bit brutal, but effective.

author: @judyofthewoods
text: That is a great method for cutting bottles. Would it work without a microwave? I live off-grid and don't have a microwave, but wondered if it could work in a similar way to your heating mats. If possible, how would I connect it up to my 12V battery, polarity wise, being that it is a circle with no beginning and end?

replies.author: @-robo-
replies.text: judyofthewoods Yes, I cut bottles with nichrome wire and this would be essentially the same. In theory, just apply the ink almost all the way round ( leaving a mm or two) and touch it with + and - at each end. The energy transfer will cause heat to build up and a crack to propogate through the glass. If you can figure out how to do that safety, you might have a viable method.

replies.author: @judyofthewoods
replies.text: Thanks Robert Martin, I will give it a go. I've heard/read about using hot wires, but that the ink does a much cleaner job (a previous video on this chanel). With my fear of glass I will probably wear welding gloves, a diving bell helmet and leather suit.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like that mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like Robert's suggestion mate

author: @nikitaredko2348
text:
Robert , off topic question - Is turning pure graphite into pyrolytic graphite a 'can do at home/garage's job? Need to be able to shape a block of pure graphite (easily available online often reasonably priced) similar to casting/machining for research purposes into diamagnetism.
The pyrolytic graphite on eBay is of limited use due to price and listed sheet/size block.

Everywhere I have been looking on currently top performing materials it's all about grain/domain orientation - magnets-neodymium,
paramagnets - 
Cold rolled grain oriented steel CRGO steel (limited supply, only available as thin sheets for transformers atm. I need to be able to cast/machine a custom shape of the stuff! Any ideas?)
Diamagnetic - pyrolytic carbon/graphite ...

Pretty sure that battery tech is affected by magnetic phenomenon away from current focus and research. 

Your experience working with carbons is invaluable mate. Hope to hear from you!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - i don't know of a way to do that 'in the kitchen' so to speak

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry about that mate- i just get too much mail these days and youtube doesn't send me a notification on every comment any more - it's about the ratio of fe2 to fe3 - you probably got that wrong  - but to be honest without actually standing over you - i can't  tell that much - all you can do is repeat it but do it more carefully - no pont telling me you did it carefully - it is mechanistic - follow the steps and you get there - cheers

replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Yeh completely understand its hard for u to say without u standing over and watching. I appreciate you getting back to me tho! I will try again! Also do you think if I put iron 3 oxide in to hydrochloric acid will I get iron 3 chloride?
